expectations.text: Explains the business has made a trading loss (revenue minus expenses)
expectations.evidence: Section 1 states 'Your apparent position: A trading loss of £2,300 before any housing costs' and Section 4 provides detailed analysis of the trading loss calculation

expectations.text: Mentions sideways loss relief as an option to offset the loss against nursing income
expectations.evidence: Option 2 in Section 4 explicitly titled 'Sideways Relief — Offset Against Your Employment Income (Most Valuable Option Here)' with detailed explanation and numerical example showing £460 tax saving

expectations.text: Distinguishes capital expenditure (laptop) from revenue expenses (subscriptions, advertising)
expectations.evidence: Section 2 clearly separates 'Laptop and Webcam (Capital Equipment)' from 'Software Subscriptions' and 'Advertising' with different tax treatment explanations for each

expectations.text: Explains WFH expense options: flat rate vs proportional actual costs
expectations.evidence: Section 3 presents 'Option A: Flat Rate (Simplified Expenses)' and 'Option B: Actual Costs (Proportionate Method)' with detailed tables and calculations for both approaches

expectations.text: Addresses the rent question with proportional calculation method (room proportion x time proportion)
expectations.evidence: Section 3 Option B explains 'Step 1 — Room proportion' and 'Step 2 — Time proportion' with explicit calculation example: '25% x 24% = 6% of your housing costs'

expectations.text: Mentions HMRC self-employment registration requirement and deadline
expectations.evidence: Section 1 states 'Register as self-employed with HMRC by 5 October 2025 (5 October following the end of the 2024/25 tax year)'
Section 1 clearly states 'File a Self Assessment tax return — online deadline is 31 January 2026; paper deadline is 31 October 2025'

expectations.text: Warns about hobby vs trade test or commercial basis requirement for loss relief
expectations.evidence: Section 4 Option 2 warns 'HMRC must be satisfied your tutoring business is a genuine trade conducted on a commercial basis with a reasonable expectation of profit — not a hobby'
